skill.name: neovim-debugging
skill.description: Debug Neovim and LazyVim configuration problems with structured hypothesis testing, headless checks, and targeted file inspection.
skill.summary: Diagnose Neovim errors, plugin loading failures, keymap issues, and LazyVim configuration problems.
skill.author: BitYoungjae

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high-severity alerts, but manual review shows the files are Markdown guidance for Neovim debugging, not executable malware. The confirmed risk is medium because the skill encourages local shell execution and inspection of hidden Neovim configuration files; publish with clear user consent expectations.

medium_findings.title: Local Shell Diagnostics Are Recommended
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run Neovim, shell, grep, git, and profiling commands. This is legitimate for debugging, but it can execute user Neovim configuration and local plugin code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files contain explicit command examples for headless Neovim and shell diagnostics. The context is debugging, not exfiltration or persistence, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Local Configuration File Inspection
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends reading hidden Neovim and LazyVim files under user configuration and plugin directories. This is needed for diagnosis, but these paths can contain private repository names, plugin state, or local settings.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly target ~/.config/nvim and ~/.local/share/nvim paths. These are normal Neovim paths, but they are still user-local hidden files.

low_findings.title: Static Cryptography And Scanner Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak crypto, certificate, network scanning, and reconnaissance alerts are caused by Markdown terminology and Neovim debugging examples. I found no implementation that performs cryptography, network scanning, or credential collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review showed documentation files and command examples, not executable malware logic. The flagged terms are mostly words such as key, LSP, paths, and debugging commands.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Require Examples Are Diagnostic Checks
low_findings.description: The dynamic require findings appear in examples that check whether Neovim Lua modules are installed or expose functions. They do not load remote code or construct module names for persistence.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The require calls are shown as user-facing diagnostics for plugin APIs. They are not part of an executable script bundled with the skill.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Manual review did not find text claiming special authority, overriding system instructions, asking the evaluator to skip analysis, or marking the skill as pre-approved.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted searches for common injection phrases found no relevant matches. The reviewed content stays within Neovim debugging instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash And Headless Neovim Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples invoke nvim, git, grep, cat, sort, tail, and profiling commands. Agents should treat these as local diagnostics and ask before broad or destructive execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are direct and numerous. They are diagnostic by intent, but still expand the runtime surface of the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hidden Neovim Directory Reads
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill tells agents to inspect ~/.config/nvim and ~/.local/share/nvim for LazyVim state and plugin files. This should be limited to files relevant to the reported issue.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The paths are explicitly listed in the documentation. They are standard for Neovim debugging, but still involve hidden user directories.

content.value_statement: Neovim and LazyVim failures are hard to isolate when plugins, keymaps, and Lua errors interact. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through focused diagnosis.
actual_capabilities: Classifies Neovim problems by symptom, including Lua errors, keymap failures, plugin loading issues, UI problems, and performance regressions.
Provides headless Neovim commands for checking plugin availability, option values, mappings, leaders, and module APIs.
Maps common Neovim Lua errors to likely causes and practical diagnostic steps.
Explains LazyVim and lazy.nvim loading behavior, extras, overrides, and plugin state checks.
Gives plugin-specific troubleshooting guidance for which-key, LSP, Treesitter, Telescope, nvim-cmp, Snacks.nvim, and LazyVim.
limitations: It is documentation guidance, not an automated Neovim repair tool.
Some checks require permission to run local shell or Neovim commands.
It cannot confirm visual UI behavior without user feedback or screenshots.
Plugin APIs may change after the documentation was written.

use_cases.title: Fix Broken Keymaps
use_cases.description: Trace whether a mapping is missing, shadowed, loaded too late, or affected by leader and localleader configuration.

use_cases.title: Debug LazyVim Plugins
use_cases.description: Check whether plugins are installed, lazy-loaded, configured with opts, or blocked by missing dependencies.

use_cases.title: Investigate Startup And Runtime Errors
use_cases.description: Interpret Lua stack traces, startup output, profiling logs, and common plugin error patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Start A Basic Diagnosis
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner user has a vague Neovim problem.
prompt_templates.prompt: My Neovim setup is failing. Ask the minimum questions needed, classify the symptom, and give me the first safe diagnostic step.

prompt_templates.title: Check A Keymap Issue
prompt_templates.scenario: A mapping does not run or does not appear in which-key.
prompt_templates.prompt: Debug why my Neovim keymap is not working. Help me determine whether the mapping exists, is shadowed, or depends on lazy loading.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze A Lua Error
prompt_templates.scenario: User has an error message or stack trace.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Neovim error patterns to explain this Lua error, identify the likely root cause, and propose a minimal verification step.

prompt_templates.title: Audit A LazyVim Plugin Setup
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user wants a structured review of plugin loading and configuration.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my LazyVim plugin setup for loading order, opts usage, dependencies, and module API assumptions. Prioritize evidence over broad checklists.

output_examples.input: My localleader mappings do not show in which-key.
output_examples.output: The response identifies localleader trigger configuration as the leading hypothesis, checks whether leader works, verifies localleader value, and suggests testing which-key manually.

output_examples.input: Neovim shows an E5108 Lua error after a plugin update.
output_examples.output: The response breaks down the stack trace, separates plugin code from user configuration, and recommends checking the caller and recent lockfile changes.

output_examples.input: Telescope is installed but a picker is missing.
output_examples.output: The response checks whether Telescope is loaded, whether the builtin picker or extension exists, and whether lazy loading prevents setup from running.

best_practices: Start with the exact symptom and test the most likely hypothesis first.
Use headless checks only when they are targeted and relevant to the user report.
Ask for interactive confirmation when the problem depends on visible UI or keypress behavior.
anti_patterns: Do not run every diagnostic command before forming a hypothesis.
Do not inspect broad configuration directories when one file or plugin is enough.
Do not assume a plugin bug before checking loading state, mappings, and recent updates.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides diagnostic commands and reasoning steps that an AI assistant may use with appropriate user consent.

faq.question: Is it only for LazyVim?
faq.answer: No. It covers general Neovim debugging, with extra guidance for LazyVim and lazy.nvim setups.

faq.question: Can it fix my configuration directly?
faq.answer: It can guide fixes and explain likely changes, but edits depend on the assistant and repository permissions.

faq.question: What kinds of errors does it understand?
faq.answer: It covers common Lua errors, missing modules, API changes, keymap failures, LSP issues, UI problems, and performance symptoms.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it recommends local shell commands and reading hidden Neovim configuration files.

faq.question: Can it diagnose plugin update regressions?
faq.answer: Yes. It suggests checking lockfile changes, plugin loading state, API changes, and rollback tests.
